title: The Holy Spirit Comes at Pentecost
objective: Understand how the Holy Spirit was poured out on the Church at Pentecost.

sections.content: After Jesus ascended into heaven, the Holy Spirit came upon the apostles at Pentecost and empowered them to proclaim the Gospel.

sections.text: And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues.
Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins.

sections.heading: The Spirit and the Church
sections.content: Pentecost marks the public beginning of the Church's mission. The Holy Spirit works through preaching and Baptism to bring people to faith in Jesus Christ.
I believe that I cannot by my own reason or strength believe in Jesus Christ, my Lord, or come to Him; but the Holy Spirit has called me by the Gospel.
sections.ref: Small Catechism — Apostles' Creed, Third Article

questions: What happened at Pentecost?
Why is the coming of the Holy Spirit so important?
How does the Holy Spirit still work through the Church today?

sections.content: Holy Spirit, create faith in our hearts through the Gospel and strengthen Your Church. Amen.

title: The Early Church
objective: Learn how the first Christians lived together in faith, worship, and fellowship.

sections.content: After Pentecost, the first Christians gathered around the apostles' teaching, fellowship, prayer, and the breaking of bread.

sections.text: And they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers.
And the Lord added to their number day by day those who were being saved.

sections.heading: Life Together in Christ
sections.content: The Church is not just a building or organization. It is the people of God gathered around His Word and Sacraments. The early Church shows what Christian community looks like under the Gospel.
The Holy Spirit calls, gathers, enlightens, and sanctifies the whole Christian Church on earth.
sections.ref: Small Catechism — Apostles' Creed, Third Article

questions: What practices shaped the life of the early Church?
Why are Word and Sacrament central to the Church?
What can Christians today learn from Acts 2?

sections.content: Lord, gather us around Your Word, strengthen our fellowship, and keep us faithful in Your Church. Amen.

title: Saul Becomes Paul
objective: Understand how Jesus called Saul and transformed him into the apostle Paul.

sections.content: Saul once persecuted Christians, but the risen Jesus appeared to him and changed his life forever.

sections.text: I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting.
He is a chosen instrument of mine to carry my name before the Gentiles.

sections.heading: Grace That Changes Lives
sections.content: Paul's conversion shows that salvation is entirely God's work. Jesus called even His enemy into faith and service, showing that no sinner is beyond His mercy.
The Holy Spirit calls by the Gospel and creates faith where there was unbelief.

questions: Who was Saul before meeting Jesus?
What does Paul's conversion teach about grace?
How does God still change lives through the Gospel?

sections.content: Lord Jesus, thank You for Your mercy toward sinners. Turn our hearts to You and use us for Your service. Amen.

title: Paul's Missionary Journeys
objective: See how the Gospel spread through the preaching of Paul and his companions.

sections.content: Paul traveled through many cities preaching Christ, planting churches, and encouraging believers.

sections.text: For I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes.
I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.

sections.heading: The Gospel Goes to the Nations
sections.content: The Christian faith was never meant for one people alone. Through Paul's journeys, God spread the message of Christ to Jews and Gentiles alike, building His Church in many places.
The Gospel is the power of God for salvation for all who believe.

questions: Why did Paul travel so much?
What challenges did he face?
Why is it important that the Gospel goes to all nations?

sections.content: Lord, bless the spread of Your Gospel throughout the world and make us bold in confessing Christ. Amen.

title: The Letters of the Apostles
objective: Learn how the apostles taught and encouraged the Church through their letters.

sections.content: The New Testament letters were written to teach believers, correct errors, and strengthen the Church in faith and love.

sections.text: Follow the pattern of the sound words that you have heard from me.
For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.

sections.heading: Teaching the Church
sections.content: The apostolic letters explain the meaning of Christ's saving work and help Christians live in faithfulness. They emphasize grace, faith, love, unity, and holy living.
We are saved by grace alone through faith in Christ alone.

questions: Why did the apostles write letters to the Church?
What truths do these letters teach?
Why does doctrine matter for Christian life?

sections.content: Lord, keep Your Church grounded in the truth of Your Word and the comfort of Your grace. Amen.

title: Christian Life and Vocation
objective: Understand how Christians live by faith and serve their neighbors in daily life.

sections.content: Christians are saved by grace, yet faith bears fruit in love, good works, and faithful service in daily life.

sections.text: But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control.
And whatever you do, in word or deed, do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus.

sections.heading: Faith Active in Love
sections.content: Good works do not earn salvation, but they do flow from faith. Christians serve God by serving their neighbors in family life, work, friendship, and every calling where God has placed them.
We should fear and love God so that we serve our neighbor in love according to His commandments.

questions: How is Christian living different from trying to earn salvation?
What is vocation?
How can faith shape everyday life?

sections.content: Lord, let our faith bear fruit in love and teach us to serve our neighbors with joy. Amen.

title: The Book of Revelation
objective: Recognize Revelation as a message of comfort and victory for the Church in Christ.

sections.content: The book of Revelation uses powerful images and visions to show that Jesus Christ reigns and that His people will share in His victory.

sections.text: Fear not, I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore.
Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing!

sections.heading: Victory in the Lamb
sections.content: Revelation is not meant to terrify believers but to strengthen them. It proclaims that Christ, the Lamb who was slain, has already won the victory. The Church can endure suffering in hope because Jesus reigns.
Christ will come again in glory to judge both the living and the dead.
sections.ref: Small Catechism — Apostles' Creed, Second Article

questions: Who stands at the center of Revelation?
Why is this book meant to comfort believers?
How does Christ's victory strengthen the Church?

sections.content: Lord Jesus, keep us faithful in hope as we wait for Your coming and rest in Your victory. Amen.

title: The New Creation
objective: Rejoice in God's promise of a new heaven and a new earth where His people will live with Him forever.

sections.content: The Bible ends with a glorious promise: God will make all things new. Sin, death, sorrow, and pain will be gone, and God's people will live with Him forever.

sections.text: He will wipe away every tear from their eyes, and death shall be no more.
Behold, I am making all things new.

sections.heading: The Hope of Eternal Life
sections.content: The story of Scripture ends where it was always headed: not with defeat, but with restoration. In the new creation, God's people will enjoy His presence forever. This is the final fulfillment of His saving plan in Christ.
On the Last Day He will raise me and all the dead, and give eternal life to me and all believers in Christ.
sections.ref: Small Catechism — Apostles' Creed, Third Article

questions: What will be different in the new creation?
Why is this the perfect ending to the Bible's story?
How does this promise give hope today?

sections.content: Lord, keep us steadfast in faith until the day You make all things new. Amen.
